title: Fixation of Eyes at UAB
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2hq08","text":"The accession and fixation of whole human eye specimens at University of Alabama Birmingham.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"ald4l","text":"1. 18-mm trephine, Stratis Healthcare, #6718L","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ev6fl","text":"2. Curved Spring Scissors, Roboz Cat# RS-5681","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e5r2m","text":"3. 4% Paraformaldehyde","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Whole eyes arrive from deceased human donors via Advancing Sight Network (Birmingham AL; formerly the Alabama Eye Bank) ≤6 hours death-to-preservation.
Fixation is performed on the globe with lens and iris in place by immersing in buffered 4% paraformaldehyde overnight.

Initially the cornea is removed with a 2-mm-wide scleral rim boundry, a circular cut is scored with an 18-mm trephine (Stratis Healthcare, #6718L) and completed with curved spring scissors (Roboz Cat# RS-5681).
To facilitate preservative penetration, without disturbing the vitreous body and detaching the retina from RPE, the iris is slit with a small incision.
The iris and lens were removed before imaging with OCT and scanning laser ophthalmoscopy (Anderson et al., 2020).
creator: Jamie Allen
